title: 5 Houseplant Hacks That Will BLOW Your Mind!
passage:
Okay, real talk — if you still think houseplants are just boring green things that sit in the corner and do nothing, you are about to be completely ___(1)___! Trust me, once you discover these game-changing tricks, you'll never look at your sad little windowsill the same way again. Ready? Let's dive right in, because number three honestly changed my whole life!

Why Your Plants Keep Dying (And The Easy Fix Nobody Tells You)

Here's the shocking truth that plant shops definitely don't want you to know: most people literally kill their plants with love. Yep, really! The number one rookie mistake is watering them way too ___(2)___, which basically drowns the poor roots until they can't breathe. Wild, right?! So do less, not more — just let that soil dry out between drinks and boom, instant green thumb.

The One Weird Trick That Changed Everything

You seriously won't believe this next one, and I didn't either! It turns out that simply giving your pot a little quarter-turn every single week keeps all that new growth beautifully ___(3)___, instead of stretching sideways and leaning toward the window like it's desperate for a tan. Mind. Blown. Honestly, your friends are going to think you're some kind of plant ___(4)___ with secret powers.

So there you have it, folks — five ridiculously simple hacks that will totally ___(5)___ your boring space into a lush green jungle. Which one are you trying first?! Smash that like button, drop a comment below, and don't you dare forget to share this with every single plant-lover you know!

questions.rationale: The hype frame 'you are about to be completely ___' followed by 'game-changing tricks' and 'you'll never look at your windowsill the same way again' demands a strong positive reaction adjective: 'amazed'. bored = polarity misfit (the opposite reaction the passage promises to overturn). informed = collocation misfit ('completely informed' does not pair with the astonishment build-up). delayed = sense misfit (not a reaction). Only 'amazed' is defensible. Language is clean; the seeded defect is REGISTER only.

The sentence explains that people 'drown' the roots and the fix is to 'do less, not more' and 'let that soil dry out', so the mistake is watering 'way too ___' = too frequently: 'often'. rarely = polarity misfit (under-watering would not drown roots). slowly = sense misfit (speed of watering is not the point). neatly = sense/collocation misfit. Only 'often' fits. Grammatical throughout; defect is REGISTER only.

The quarter-turn 'keeps all that new growth beautifully ___, instead of stretching sideways and leaning toward the window' — the contrast with 'sideways ... leaning' fixes the gap as balanced/uniform growth: 'even'. sideways = contradiction (that is the explicitly rejected outcome). wilted = sense misfit (negative, and not what rotation produces). cheap = sense misfit (not a growth descriptor). Only 'even' is defensible. Clean English; REGISTER-only defect.

'Your friends are going to think you're some kind of plant ___ with secret powers' is admiring, so the noun must name a master: 'expert'. beginner = polarity misfit (the opposite of the admiration expressed). disaster = polarity misfit (insult, not praise). vegetable = sense misfit (wrong referent). Only 'expert' fits the flattering frame. Grammatical; the defect is REGISTER only.

'Five ... hacks that will totally ___ your boring space into a lush green jungle' takes a verb of change taking 'X into Y': 'transform'. ignore = sense misfit (cannot 'ignore X into Y'). abandon = sense/grammar misfit (does not take the 'into' resultative here). purchase = sense misfit (you do not purchase a space into a jungle). Only 'transform' is idiomatic. Language is clean; the seeded defect is REGISTER only.

generator_meta.gap_marker_convention: ___(n)___ inline in passage; questions[] carries gap n as q_index n with prompt 'Gap (n)'
generator_meta.schema_note: 5 gaps => 5 questions[]; matches the cloze invariant and M-SCHEMA's 1-5 range and bands.json cloze prompt/option classes.

_seed.defect: Non-HP passage register: a structurally VALID 5-gap ELF cloze (correct schema, gaps ___(1)___..___(5)___, four single-word options per gap, exactly one defensible key each, grammatical sentence-level English) whose PASSAGE is unmistakably clickbait/blog listicle — direct reader address ('you are about to be completely amazed', 'Trust me', 'You seriously won't believe this next one'), hype exclamation marks, SEO-flavored subheads baked into the prose ('Why Your Plants Keep Dying (And The Easy Fix Nobody Tells You)', 'The One Weird Trick That Changed Everything'), and a social-CTA sign-off ('Smash that like button, drop a comment below ... share this'). No authentic ELF magazine/journal excerpt reads this way.
defect_locations: title: '5 Houseplant Hacks That Will BLOW Your Mind!'

_seed.scope_assertion: This seed also asserts the G-REGISTER cloze-format scope rule (owner decision 2026-07-24) does NOT exempt register: the gap FORMAT (numbered gaps, single-word option sets) is out of jurisdiction and must not be a finding, but the PASSAGE register is fully in jurisdiction. The kill must cite the passage's clickbait/blog genre against the ELF exemplars — NOT the cloze format. Because keys are solvable (no G-KEY kill) and the English is grammatical (no G-ENG kill), a DEAD verdict here proves G-REGISTER fired on passage register, and that the scope exemption does not rubber-stamp cloze content.
